programs.name: Community Care Services Program (CCSP)
programs.tagline: Home care services to help your loved one avoid nursing home placement while staying on Medicaid

localEntities.name: Area Agencies on Aging (contact via 866-552-4464 for local)

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 65+ and needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication management, CCSP provides home care services as an alternative to nursing home placement. The program covers personal care, meal preparation, light housekeeping, respite care, and home-delivered meals, all while your loved one stays in their own home.

Because CCSP is a Medicaid waiver program, it follows nursing home financial eligibility rules. Your loved one must qualify for intermediate nursing facility level of care (a clinical assessment of whether they need daily assistance with basic activities). Services are limited to the average annual cost of Medicaid nursing facility care in Georgia.

The program operates through 12 regional Area Agencies on Aging statewide, but waitlists are common because the program prioritizes those with the greatest need.

summary: Need daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication
Meet Medicaid nursing home income limits

exemptAssets: Primary home (up to $752,000 equity if living there)
Home if spouse or dependent relative lives there

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must require intermediate Nursing Facility Level of Care, assessed through functional evaluation of daily activities like mobility, eating, toileting, meal preparation, and money management
otherRequirements: Georgia resident
Choose community-based care over nursing home
Physician approval of care plan
Can live safely in community with services
Participate in only one waiver program at a time

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional Area Agency on Aging for functional assessment, Medicaid application comes after CCSP evaluation.

steps.title: Call for initial screening
steps.description: Call 1-866-55-AGING (866-552-4464) to reach your regional Area Agency on Aging. They will conduct intake screening and determine if your loved one meets basic eligibility.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: Schedule Determination of Need Functional Assessment-Revised (DON-R) and Minimum Data Set Home Care (MDS-HC) to evaluate your loved one's need for daily assistance with activities.

steps.title: Get physician approval
steps.description: Your loved one's doctor must approve the care plan and confirm they need intermediate nursing facility level of care.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: After CCSP assessment, apply for Medicaid through your county Department of Family and Children Services office.

steps.title: Wait for prioritization
steps.description: If approved, you may be placed on a waitlist. The program prioritizes those with the greatest unmet care needs.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified, depends on assessment scheduling and Medicaid processing
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible, program prioritizes those most in need
applicationGuide.tip: Start with the functional assessment even if you're not sure about Medicaid eligibility, the evaluation determines if your loved one qualifies medically before financial review.

urls.label: Apply for Elderly and Disabled Waiver

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for your loved one and spouse
Medicare card (both parts A and B)
Proof of Georgia residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements)
Life insurance policies with face values
Vehicle titles and registration
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting functional impairment
Physician statement confirming need for intermediate nursing facility level of care
Verification of citizenship or legal residency

contacts.label: Georgia Area Agencies on Aging
contacts.description: Primary contact for CCSP assessment and intake

contacts.label: Coastal Georgia AAA (example region)
contacts.description: Regional contact for coastal counties

contacts.label: Georgia 2-1-1
contacts.description: General social services helpline for referrals

applicationNotes: You may be potentially Medicaid eligible only after CCSP admission, the functional assessment comes before financial eligibility
The home may be subject to Medicaid Estate Recovery after your loved one's death, even though it's exempt during benefits
Regional variations exist in providers and waitlist times, some Area Agencies have more immediate availability than others
Your loved one must choose between CCSP and other waiver programs, they cannot participate in multiple waivers simultaneously
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than $752,000?
faqs.answer: No, if your loved one lives in the home or intends to return, home equity cannot exceed $752,000 in 2026. However, the home is completely exempt if a spouse, dependent child, or certain relatives live there regardless of value.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one needs more care than CCSP provides?
faqs.answer: CCSP services are capped at the average annual cost of nursing facility care in Georgia. If your loved one's needs exceed this limit, they may need to transition to nursing home care or supplement with private-pay services.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Waitlist times vary by region and your loved one's priority level based on unmet care needs. Those with the greatest functional impairment and least family support get priority. Contact your regional Area Agency on Aging at 866-552-4464 for current wait times.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can initiate the application process. However, your loved one must participate in the functional assessment, and you may need power of attorney or guardianship to complete financial portions of the Medicaid application.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get CCSP and other Medicaid waiver services at the same time?
faqs.answer: No, your loved one can only participate in one waiver program at a time. They must choose between CCSP and other Georgia Medicaid waivers like the Comprehensive Supports Waiver.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is slightly over the Medicaid limit?
faqs.answer: SSI recipients automatically qualify financially. For others, Georgia follows complex Medicaid nursing facility income rules. Contact your county Department of Family and Children Services to determine eligibility based on your loved one's specific situation.

faqs.question: Does CCSP cover 24-hour care?
faqs.answer: No, CCSP provides intermittent services like personal care visits, meal delivery, and respite care. If your loved one needs 24-hour supervision, they may not be appropriate for community-based care and might need nursing facility placement.

faqs.question: What happens to my loved one's estate after they die?
faqs.answer: Georgia may pursue Medicaid Estate Recovery to recoup costs from your loved one's estate, including the home. However, recovery is postponed if a surviving spouse lives in the home, and there are other protections for certain family members.

programs.name: Home & Community-Based Services Program
programs.tagline: Get personal care, homemaker services, and adult day care so your loved one can stay home instead of entering a nursing home.

localEntities.name: Local Area Agency on Aging offices

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs help with daily tasks like bathing, dressing, or meal preparation but wants to avoid nursing home placement, Georgia's Home & Community-Based Services Program provides in-home personal care, homemaker services, adult day care, and respite care. Your loved one must be 65+, qualify for Georgia Medicaid, and meet nursing home level of care requirements through a clinical assessment.

Because this is a Medicaid waiver program with enrollment caps, there are often waiting lists. The program prioritizes those with the greatest need first. A registered nurse will visit your loved one's home to conduct the required Minimum Data Set Home Care (MDS-HC) assessment to determine if they need daily help with activities like transferring, mobility, eating, toileting, or have cognitive issues like dementia.

While your loved one can keep their home (if equity is under $752,000 in 2026), Georgia will pursue Estate Recovery after death to recoup costs from the estate, including the home. The 60-month look-back rule penalizes any asset transfers below fair market value made in the five years before applying.

summary: Meet nursing home level of care
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet Nursing Facility Level of Care through MDS-HC assessment showing impairments in daily activities (transferring, mobility, eating, toileting, meal prep, money management, housework) or cognitive issues like dementia
otherRequirements: Must qualify for Georgia Medicaid first
Must be Georgia resident

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging to start the Medicaid application process, then schedule the required in-home nursing assessment.

steps.title: Apply for Georgia Medicaid
steps.description: Contact your local Area Agency on Aging or Department of Community Health to begin the Medicaid application process. You must qualify for Medicaid before accessing HCBS services.

steps.title: Request HCBS assessment
steps.description: Once Medicaid-eligible, request the Home & Community-Based Services assessment through your Area Agency on Aging.

steps.title: Complete MDS-HC assessment
steps.description: A registered nurse will visit your loved one's home to conduct the Minimum Data Set Home Care assessment to determine if they meet nursing home level of care requirements.

steps.title: Wait for approval and enrollment
steps.description: If approved, you'll be placed on the enrollment list. Due to caps on enrollment, there may be a waiting period before services begin.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Often waiting lists due to enrollment caps set by the state
applicationGuide.tip: Apply early and be prepared for potential wait times. Priority goes to those with the greatest documented need.

urls.label: Georgia Department of Community Health HCBS

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for applicant and spouse
Medicare card (both parts A and B)
Birth certificate or passport for proof of age
Proof of Georgia residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities, trust agreements)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements)
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with face values
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting functional limitations
Current medication list from physician
Documentation of existing home care services

contacts.label: Georgia Department of Community Health
contacts.description: Primary state agency for HCBS program information

contacts.label: Georgia 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline to connect with local Area Agency on Aging

applicationNotes: You cannot apply directly for HCBS - you must first qualify for Georgia Medicaid
The MDS-HC assessment must be conducted in person at your loved one's home by a registered nurse
Don't confuse this with non-Medicaid HCBS programs for seniors 60+ that use sliding scale fees
Estate Recovery will pursue reimbursement from your loved one's estate after death, including the home

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, your loved one can keep their primary home as long as the equity doesn't exceed $752,000 in 2026 and they live there or intend to return. However, Georgia will pursue Estate Recovery after death to recoup program costs from the home and other estate assets.

faqs.question: What if my loved one needs help but doesn't meet nursing home level of care?
faqs.answer: This program is only for seniors who meet nursing home level of care requirements through the MDS-HC assessment. If your loved one needs lighter assistance, look into Georgia's non-Medicaid HCBS programs for seniors 60+ that use sliding scale fees based on income.

faqs.question: How long is the waiting list really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary by region and are not specified by the state. Because Georgia caps enrollment in this program, waiting lists are common. Priority goes to those with the greatest documented need first.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can apply on behalf of a loved one with cognitive impairment. You may need to become their authorized representative or have power of attorney to complete the application and make decisions about their care.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one transfers assets to qualify?
faqs.answer: Georgia looks back 60 months for any asset transfers below fair market value. Transfers during this period will create a penalty period where your loved one is ineligible for services, even if they otherwise qualify.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get both Medicare home health and HCBS services?
faqs.answer: Yes, but the services can't duplicate each other. Medicare covers skilled nursing and therapy visits, while HCBS covers personal care, homemaker services, and adult day care that Medicare doesn't cover.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's needs change after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Your loved one will have periodic reassessments to determine if their care needs have changed. Services can be increased if needs grow, or decreased if they improve, based on the updated MDS-HC assessment.

faqs.question: Do I have to choose between this program and nursing home care?
faqs.answer: No, if your loved one's needs become too great for home-based care, they can transition to nursing home care covered by Medicaid. The goal is to delay or prevent nursing home placement as long as safely possible.

programs.name: Georgia Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Get help paying Medicare premiums and costs if your loved one's income is below $1,660/month

localEntities.name: Local Department of Family & Children Services (DFCS) offices; SHIP counselors statewide

programs.intro:
If your loved one has Medicare and limited income, Georgia's Medicare Savings Programs can pay their Medicare premiums and out-of-pocket costs. The three programs, QMB, SLMB, and QI, save between $2,435 and $6,000+ per year by covering Medicare Part B premiums ($202.90/month in 2026) and, for QMB enrollees, all Medicare deductibles and copayments.

QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary) provides the most help for parents earning up to $1,325/month ($1,783 for couples). SLMB and QI cover only the Part B premium for slightly higher incomes up to $1,660/month for individuals. Because QMB enrollees cannot be billed by providers for Medicare cost-sharing, it works like comprehensive Medigap coverage. All three programs automatically qualify your loved one for Extra Help with prescription drug costs.

These programs fill the gap for parents who earn too much for full Medicaid but struggle with Medicare costs. Apply early in the year for QI, funding is limited and eligible applicants can be denied once the federal allocation runs out.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premium savings ($2,435/year) plus Medicare cost-sharing for QMB enrollees

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

otherRequirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A
U.S. citizen or qualified immigrant
Georgia resident
Not eligible for full Medicaid

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through Georgia Gateway online or call SHIP at 1-866-552-4464, processing typically takes 45 days.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect Medicare card, Social Security award letters, bank statements from last 3 months, and proof of Georgia residency before starting your application.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online at Georgia Gateway (gateway.ga.gov) using the Medicaid application, call Georgia SHIP at 1-866-552-4464 (Option 4), or visit your local DFCS office.

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Use the Georgia Medicaid Application (J2F form) and select Medicare Savings Program. Include all required documentation to avoid delays.

steps.title: Wait for eligibility determination
steps.description: Processing takes up to 45 days. SLMB and QI can provide up to 3 months retroactive coverage with premium refunds if approved.

steps.title: Receive benefits
steps.description: QMB benefits start the month after approval. SLMB and QI members receive Medicare Part B premium payments directly to Medicare.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Up to 45 days from complete application
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI may have waitlist or reach funding cap during the year, apply early
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QI early in the calendar year, it's funded by a limited federal block grant and eligible people can be denied once funds run out.

documentsNeeded: Medicare card (showing Parts A and B)
Social Security award letter or SSI award letter
Pay stubs from last 3 months (if working)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment account statements (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Birth certificate or proof of age
Social Security card
Proof of Georgia residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Proof of U.S. citizenship or qualified immigrant status
Life insurance policies with face values
Vehicle titles and registration
Property documents (deeds, tax statements) if you own real estate
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Documentation of any other income sources

contacts.label: Georgia SHIP (Medicare Help)
contacts.description: Press Option 4 for Medicare Savings Program assistance

contacts.label: Georgia Medicaid Customer Service
contacts.description: Questions about Medicaid applications and eligibility

contacts.label: Georgia 2-1-1
contacts.description: General assistance finding local DFCS offices and social services

applicationNotes: QI funding is limited by federal block grant, eligible applicants may be denied if funds are exhausted for the year
Income and asset limits update every April 1, confirm current figures at medicaid.georgia.gov before applying
QMB protects your loved one from provider billing for Medicare cost-sharing, but providers can still bill for non-Medicare covered services
Asset limits include your loved one's spouse's assets even if the spouse is not applying for the program
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

contentSections.title: Program Comparison: QMB vs SLMB vs QI

content.benefits: Pays Part B premium ($202.90/month) + ALL Medicare deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments. Providers cannot bill you.

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, the primary home is completely exempt from asset limits regardless of value. Only countable assets like bank accounts, stocks, and second vehicles count toward the $9,090 individual/$13,630 couple limit.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one gets approved for QMB and a provider still sends bills?
faqs.answer: Providers cannot legally bill QMB enrollees for Medicare deductibles, coinsurance, or copayments. Contact the provider's billing department with your loved one's QMB card, and if they continue billing, report them to Georgia Medicaid at 1-877-423-4746.

faqs.question: Can I apply for Medicare Savings Programs and SNAP at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply for both programs simultaneously through Georgia Gateway or your local DFCS office. Both use similar income documentation, which can speed up the process.

faqs.question: How long is the QI waitlist really?
faqs.answer: QI doesn't have a traditional waitlist, instead, eligible applicants are denied once the federal funding allocation runs out for the year. This typically happens later in the calendar year, so apply by March or April for the best chance.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income changes after they're approved?
faqs.answer: Report income changes to Georgia Medicaid within 30 days. Small increases might move them between programs (QMB to SLMB to QI) rather than losing benefits entirely. Annual redetermination reviews happen automatically.

faqs.question: Can a family member apply on behalf of an elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can apply as authorized representatives. You'll need to complete additional paperwork establishing your authority to act on their behalf, or have legal documents like power of attorney.

faqs.question: Does Extra Help for prescription drugs happen automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, all Medicare Savings Program enrollees automatically qualify for Medicare Part D Extra Help (Low Income Subsidy). Medicare will notify your loved one directly about this additional benefit, which can save $1,000+ annually on prescription costs.

faqs.question: What if my loved one has Medicare Advantage instead of Original Medicare?
faqs.answer: Medicare Savings Programs work with both Original Medicare and Medicare Advantage plans. The program pays the Medicare Part B premium regardless of which Medicare option your loved one chose, and QMB covers cost-sharing for Medicare-covered services.

programs.name: Senior Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (Senior SNAP)
programs.tagline: Grocery assistance for Georgia seniors 60+ who live on fixed income only

localEntities.name: Administered by Georgia Division of Family & Children Services (DFCS); apply via central phone/email, no regional offices specified for Senior SNAP

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and lives only on fixed income like Social Security or retirement benefits, they may qualify for monthly grocery assistance through Senior SNAP. This streamlined version of food stamps provides benefits loaded onto an EBT card (works like a debit card at grocery stores) with simplified application requirements for seniors.

Because Senior SNAP is designed for seniors on fixed income, your loved one cannot have any earned income from work, only Social Security, SSI, retirement, VA benefits, or disability payments qualify. Income limits are $1,695/month for a single person or $2,291/month for a couple (130% of federal poverty level). The age requirement increases from 60+ to 66+ starting February 2, 2026.
programs.savingsSource: Monthly grocery benefits based on household income

summary: Fixed income only - no work earnings
otherRequirements: No earned income from work (including excluded types)
Fixed income only: Social Security, SSI, retirement, VA benefits, Railroad Retirement
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen (5+ years residency, disability benefits, or child under 18)
All household members must meet age/no-work criteria and share food preparation

applicationGuide.summary: Call to request an application by mail, no office visits or face-to-face interviews required.

steps.title: Request application
steps.description: Call (404) 370-6236 to request a Senior SNAP application be mailed to you, or email seniorsnap@dhr.state.ga.us

steps.title: Complete and return
steps.description: Fill out the mailed application and return it to Georgia DFCS at the address provided with your application packet

steps.title: Provide documents
steps.description: Submit required verification documents, fixed income is often auto-verified through computer matches, so minimal paperwork may be needed

applicationGuide.tip: Because this is for seniors on fixed income, the verification process is streamlined, Social Security and other benefits are often verified automatically without needing additional paperwork.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID
Social Security card
Proof of citizenship or qualified non-citizen status
Social Security award letter (if not auto-verified)
SSI award letter (if applicable)
Retirement or pension income statements
VA benefits award letter (if applicable)
Railroad Retirement documentation (if applicable)
Proof of Georgia residency (utility bill, lease, or state document)
Medical expense receipts (if reporting medical costs)
Shelter expense documentation (rent receipt, mortgage statement, property tax)
Bank statements for any accounts with more than $4,500 total

contacts.label: Senior SNAP Application Line
contacts.description: Request Senior SNAP application by mail

contacts.label: Georgia 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline for all social services and benefits

applicationNotes: Age requirement changes to 66+ effective February 2, 2026, current 60+ seniors remain eligible
If your loved one has any work earnings, they're not eligible for Senior SNAP but can apply for regular SNAP with medical expense deductions
Computer matches often auto-verify Social Security and other fixed income, you may not need to submit additional income proof
All household members must meet the age and no-work requirements and share food preparation
relatedPrograms: Regular SNAP (if ineligible for Senior SNAP)

faqs.question: What happens when the age requirement changes to 66 in February 2026?
faqs.answer: Seniors already enrolled at age 60+ can continue receiving benefits. The new 66+ age requirement only applies to new applicants starting February 2, 2026. Current recipients won't lose benefits due to the age change.

faqs.question: Can my loved one work part-time and still get Senior SNAP?
faqs.answer: No. Senior SNAP prohibits any earned income from work, even part-time or excluded types. If your loved one has work earnings, they should apply for regular SNAP instead, which allows medical expense deductions that may increase benefits.

faqs.question: What if my loved one receives both Social Security and a small pension?
faqs.answer: Multiple fixed income sources are allowed as long as there's no work earnings. Social Security, pensions, retirement benefits, and VA payments can be combined. The total monthly income must stay under the limits: $1,695 for one person or $2,291 for a couple.

faqs.question: How much will my loved one receive in grocery benefits?
faqs.answer: Benefit amounts depend on household size and net income. Generally, every $100 more in net income reduces benefits by about $30. The exact amount follows standard SNAP benefit calculations but varies by individual circumstances.

faqs.question: Can I apply for Senior SNAP on behalf of my loved one?
faqs.answer: The application process allows representatives, but you'll need proper legal documentation. Call (404) 370-6236 to discuss your situation and what legal documents are required to apply on your loved one's behalf.

faqs.question: What if my loved one gets denied for Senior SNAP?
faqs.answer: If denied due to work income or age requirements, your loved one can apply for regular SNAP benefits through the standard process. Regular SNAP has different rules and allows medical expense deductions that might increase benefit amounts.

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Get $350-400 applied directly to your loved one's energy bills if they're on a limited income.

localEntities.name: Local Community Action Agencies (CAAs), e.g., Community Action For Improvement (https://www.cafi-ga.org), Enrichment Services (https://enrichmentservices.org), Fulton Atlanta Community Action Authority (https://www.facaa.org), Southwest Georgia Community Action Council (https://www.swgacac.com). Full list via Georgia DFCS

programs.intro:
If your loved one struggles with high heating or cooling bills, LIHEAP can provide $350-400 paid directly to their energy company each year. Because the program prioritizes seniors age 60+, your loved one gets first access to appointment slots and receives the maximum $400 benefit amount.

The program covers one heating benefit and one cooling benefit per year, with payments going straight to the utility company, your loved one never handles the money directly. Because each county has its own Community Action Agency with separate budgets and waitlists, availability varies significantly by location.

Your loved one's energy company must be a registered LIHEAP vendor, and household income must be at or below 60% of Georgia's median income for their household size. Applications require scheduled appointments and operate first-come, first-served until county funds run out.
programs.savingsSource: Direct payments to energy suppliers, with seniors 60+ receiving maximum $400

summary: Income at or below 60% of state median
Responsible for paying home energy bills
Energy company must be registered LIHEAP vendor
otherRequirements: Responsible for paying the cost of energy for the primary home heating source
U.S. citizen or lawfully admitted immigrant
Energy supplier must be a registered LIHEAP vendor with Georgia Department of Human Services
Must apply in county of residence

applicationGuide.summary: Schedule an appointment with your county's Community Action Agency, seniors 60+ get priority scheduling starting in January.

steps.title: Find your county's Community Action Agency
steps.description: Visit dfcs.georgia.gov to locate your county's specific CAA. Each county has its own agency, budget, and phone number.

steps.title: Schedule an appointment
steps.description: Call your local CAA or check their online portal for appointment scheduling. Seniors 60+ typically get priority access starting January 2nd.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income for all household members (last 30 days), Social Security cards, most recent gas and electric bills, and valid photo ID.

steps.title: Attend your appointment
steps.description: Meet with your local CAA representative. If you're homebound, ask about home visits, many agencies provide this service.

steps.title: Payment goes directly to utility
steps.description: If approved, LIHEAP pays your energy company directly. You'll see the credit on your next bill.

applicationGuide.processingTime: First-come, first-served basis with no specific timeline provided
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, if appointment slots are full, you can join a waitlist or check back periodically. Wait times vary significantly by county funding.
applicationGuide.tip: Call early in the program year and ask specifically about senior priority dates, many counties open applications for 60+ or 65+ residents before general enrollment.

documentsNeeded: Valid U.S. issued photo ID
Social Security cards for all household members
Proof of income for all household members 18+ from the last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letters, pension statements)
Zero-income form if anyone has no income (completed at appointment)
Most recent gas bill showing name, address, account number, and amount due
Most recent electric bill showing name, address, account number, and amount due
Verification of age if required for priority consideration
Signed letter if a representative is applying on behalf of the household member

contacts.label: Georgia 2-1-1
contacts.description: Statewide helpline for all social services and local CAA contact information

contacts.label: Georgia DFCS
contacts.description: Visit dfcs.georgia.gov for complete list of county Community Action Agencies

applicationNotes: Seniors 60+ receive priority scheduling and the maximum $400 benefit amount
You cannot switch counties if your local agency runs out of funds, you must apply where you live
Energy suppliers must be registered LIHEAP vendors or you'll be ineligible, regardless of income
Bring copies of all documents to your appointment, originals may be required for verification

faqs.question: What if my loved one's energy company isn't a registered LIHEAP vendor?
faqs.answer: Your loved one cannot receive LIHEAP benefits, even if they meet all income requirements. Contact your local Community Action Agency to confirm which companies are registered vendors in your area before applying.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get both heating and cooling assistance in the same year?
faqs.answer: Yes, if funding is available. LIHEAP provides one heating benefit and one cooling benefit per program year. Cooling assistance is seasonal and depends on your county's available budget.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's county runs out of money before their appointment?
faqs.answer: They cannot switch to another county's program, LIHEAP requires you to apply where you live. They'll need to join the waitlist or wait for the next program year when new federal funding becomes available.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you'll need a signed letter authorizing you to represent them. Some agencies also offer home visits for homebound applicants, which might be easier than having you serve as a representative.

faqs.question: How does the 60% state median income limit work for different household sizes?
faqs.answer: Income limits vary by household size, but specific dollar amounts aren't published online. Your local Community Action Agency will have the current income table and can tell you the exact limit for your loved one's household size over the phone.

faqs.question: What if my loved one has zero income?
faqs.answer: They can still apply. You'll need to complete a zero-income form at the appointment to document their situation. Having no income doesn't automatically disqualify them if other household members have income below the limits.

faqs.question: When do applications typically open each year?
faqs.answer: Timing varies by county, but many agencies prioritize seniors starting January 2nd, with general applications opening February 2nd. Call your local Community Action Agency in December to confirm their specific schedule.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for LIHEAP and other utility assistance programs at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, LIHEAP can be combined with Georgia Power CARE and other utility assistance programs. The agencies typically coordinate to maximize your loved one's benefits without duplication.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.tagline: Free home energy improvements to reduce your loved one's utility bills if they qualify by income.

localEntities.name: Local providers listed by county at https://gefa.georgia.gov/weatherization

programs.intro:
If your loved one's income is at or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Level (about $30,120/year for one person in 2026), they may qualify for free home energy efficiency improvements through Georgia's Weatherization Assistance Program. Because this program provides services based on an energy audit, your loved one gets customized improvements like air sealing, insulation, HVAC upgrades, and energy-efficient lighting at no cost.

The program serves both homeowners and renters (renters need landlord permission). Because elderly households receive priority, your loved one may move through the process faster than younger applicants. The improvements are designed to reduce monthly utility bills, though savings vary based on the home's current condition and which improvements are installed.

summary: Income at or below 200% Federal Poverty Level
otherRequirements: Must live in single-family home or manufactured home (campers and non-stationary trailers ineligible)
Renters need written landlord permission
Program does not fix structural issues like roofing, walls, or flooring holes

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your county's community action agency by phone, processing includes income verification, energy audit, then contractor installation.

steps.title: Find your county's agency
steps.description: Visit https://gefa.georgia.gov/weatherization to find your local community action agency. Examples: CSRA EOA serves Richmond, Columbia, and surrounding counties at (706) 945-1616. Action Pact serves Savannah/Chatham County.

steps.title: Call to request application
steps.description: Contact your local agency by phone. Some agencies like CSRA EOA also offer downloadable applications at www.csraeoa.org. Be prepared for potential waitlist due to high demand.

steps.title: Submit income verification
steps.description: Provide proof your household income is at or below 200% Federal Poverty Level, or proof of Supplemental Security Income (SSI) receipt. Include proof of residency and landlord permission if renting.

steps.title: Schedule energy audit
steps.description: Once qualified, an energy auditor will assess your loved one's home to determine which improvements will provide the most energy savings.

steps.title: Contractor installation
steps.description: Approved contractors install improvements at no cost based on audit results, may include air sealing, insulation, HVAC work, or energy-efficient lighting.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Processing time varies by local agency and waitlist length
applicationGuide.waitlist: Due to high demand, there may be a waiting list
applicationGuide.tip: Elderly households receive priority, so mention your loved one's age when calling.

documentsNeeded: Proof of household income for last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letter, pension statements)
Social Security cards for all household members
Proof of residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or mortgage statement)
Written landlord permission letter (renters only)
Birth certificates or other proof of age for household members
SSI award letter if qualifying through SSI receipt
Most recent electric and gas utility bills
Property deed or lease agreement showing home type eligibility

contacts.label: Georgia Weatherization Directory
contacts.description: County-by-county listing of local agencies

contacts.label: CSRA Economic Opportunity Authority
contacts.description: Serves Richmond, Columbia, and surrounding CSRA counties

contacts.label: Georgia 2-1-1
contacts.description: Help finding local services and programs

applicationNotes: Priority given to elderly households, families with children, and households with disabilities, mention these factors when applying
Renters must get landlord agreement before applying, some landlords are hesitant because they benefit from improvements at no cost
Program cannot fix pre-existing structural problems, if your loved one's home needs major roof, wall, or foundation repairs, address those separately
Each county uses different community action agencies, so you must contact the specific agency for your loved one's county
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they rent their home?
faqs.answer: Yes, renters qualify but need written permission from their landlord. Because the improvements increase property value at no cost to the landlord, many agree once they understand the program. Contact your local agency if the landlord has questions about the process.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's home needs major structural repairs first?
faqs.answer: The program cannot fix pre-existing structural issues like roof damage, wall holes, or foundation problems. These must be addressed separately before weatherization work begins. The energy audit will identify if structural repairs are needed first.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Waitlist length varies by county and local agency capacity. Because elderly households receive priority, your loved one may move through faster than younger applicants. Call your local agency for current wait times in your area.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help with the application process. You'll need your loved one's consent and may need to provide documentation showing you're authorized to act on their behalf for some steps in the process.

faqs.question: What if my loved one receives SSI but their total household income seems too high?
faqs.answer: SSI recipients automatically qualify regardless of other household income. Provide the SSI award letter as proof of eligibility instead of calculating total household income against the Federal Poverty Level limits.

faqs.question: Will the improvements actually reduce utility bills?
faqs.answer: Energy savings vary based on your loved one's home condition and which improvements are installed. The program focuses on cost-effective measures identified through the energy audit, but cannot guarantee specific dollar savings amounts.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for both weatherization and LIHEAP energy assistance?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate programs and your loved one can apply for both. LIHEAP helps with current energy bills while weatherization makes long-term improvements to reduce future bills. Contact the same local agencies for both programs.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different county after applying?
faqs.answer: You'll need to restart the application process with the community action agency in the new county. Weatherization improvements stay with the property, not the person, so completed work benefits the home's future occupants.

programs.name: Georgia State Health Insurance Assistance Program (Georgia SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free Medicare counseling to help your loved one choose the right plan and avoid costly mistakes.

localEntities.name: Statewide helpline; local examples: Athens (706-549-4850, lplatter@accaging.org)

programs.intro:
If your loved one has Medicare or is turning 65, Georgia SHIP provides free, unbiased counseling to help them navigate Medicare choices, appeals, and enrollment. Trained volunteers help with everything from choosing between Medicare Advantage and Original Medicare to understanding prescription drug coverage and resolving billing disputes.

Georgia SHIP counselors also help your loved one apply for money-saving programs like Medicare Savings Programs (which can cover Medicare premiums for people earning up to $1,478/month) and Extra Help for prescription drugs. Because these counselors don't sell insurance, they'll give you honest guidance based on your loved one's specific health needs and budget.

otherRequirements: Must be a Medicare beneficiary or caregiver/family member of one
Georgia resident

applicationGuide.summary: Call the statewide helpline at 1-866-552-4464 (option 4) to schedule free counseling, no application needed.

steps.title: Call the Georgia SHIP helpline
steps.description: Call 1-866-552-4464 and select option 4. Lines are open Monday-Friday 8am-5pm.

steps.title: Schedule your counseling session
steps.description: A volunteer counselor will schedule a phone or in-person appointment based on your preference and location.

steps.title: Prepare your Medicare documents
steps.description: Gather your loved one's Medicare card, recent medical bills, and any Medicare Summary Notices for a more personalized session.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Services provided immediately upon contact during business hours
applicationGuide.tip: Have your loved one's Medicare number ready when you call, it helps counselors provide more specific guidance.

contacts.label: Georgia SHIP Helpline
contacts.description: Statewide Medicare counseling (select option 4)

contacts.label: Athens Area Office
contacts.description: Local SHIP services for Athens region

faqs.question: Can I call on behalf of my loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members and caregivers can call Georgia SHIP for guidance. However, if you need to make changes to your loved one's Medicare plans or access their personal information, you'll need to be their authorized representative through Medicare first.

faqs.question: Will SHIP counselors try to sell me insurance?
faqs.answer: No. Georgia SHIP counselors are trained volunteers who don't sell insurance or get paid by insurance companies. They provide unbiased information to help you compare all available options based on your loved one's specific needs.

faqs.question: Can SHIP help if my loved one is already enrolled in Medicare Advantage?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors help with ongoing Medicare issues including plan changes during Open Enrollment, appeals and grievances, billing problems, and understanding coverage. They can also help evaluate whether your loved one should switch to Original Medicare.

faqs.question: What if my loved one makes too much for Medicaid but struggles with Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors specialize in programs like Medicare Savings Programs (for people earning up to $1,478/month single) and Extra Help for prescription drugs (up to about $1,800/month single). Many people don't know these programs exist.

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals
programs.tagline: Get nutritious meals delivered to your loved one's home if they're 60+ and can't shop or cook safely.
programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggles to shop for groceries or prepare meals safely, Georgia's Home-Delivered Meals program brings nutritious meals directly to their door. Most participants receive 5 meals per week (Monday through Friday) at no charge, each meal provides at least one-third of daily nutritional needs following USDA guidelines.

The program prioritizes homebound seniors with functional impairments (difficulty bathing, dressing, or managing medications), high nutrition risk, or food insecurity. Because this runs through 12 regional Area Agencies on Aging, meal providers and delivery schedules vary by county, some areas use Open Hand Atlanta, others use Mom's Meals or GA Foods through health plans.

After 20 consecutive meals, your loved one will be reassessed and may be placed on a waiting list to make room for others in crisis. Voluntary contributions are welcomed but not required.

summary: Unable to shop or prepare meals
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be homebound with functional impairments preventing shopping or meal preparation, assessed using the DON-R instrument (measures ability to perform daily activities like bathing, dressing, medication management)
otherRequirements: Moderate to high nutrition risk
Food insecurity may qualify
Spouses of any age if married to someone 60+

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Area Agency on Aging for a phone assessment, no forms needed upfront.

steps.title: Find your local Area Agency on Aging
steps.description: Call the Eldercare Locator at 1-800-677-1116 or visit Eldercare.acl.gov to get your county's AAA contact information.

steps.title: Request a needs assessment
steps.description: Call your AAA to request home-delivered meals. They'll conduct a phone assessment covering functional abilities, nutrition risk, and food security.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: Answer questions about your loved one's ability to shop, cook, bathe, dress, and manage medications. This DON-R assessment determines priority level.

steps.title: Get matched with local provider
steps.description: If approved, your AAA will connect you with the meal delivery service in your area (Open Hand Atlanta, Mom's Meals, GA Foods, or other local provider).

steps.title: Start receiving meals
steps.description: Meals typically begin within 1-2 weeks and arrive Monday through Friday. Delivery includes a wellness check and nutrition education.

applicationGuide.waitlist: After 20 consecutive meals, reassessment required and may be placed on waiting list
applicationGuide.tip: If your loved one has Medicaid or Medicare Advantage, contact their case manager first, they may have faster access through existing care coordination.

contacts.label: Eldercare Locator
contacts.description: Find your local Area Agency on Aging

contacts.label: Georgia Division of Aging Services
contacts.description: State program information and AAA contacts

applicationNotes: No formal application forms, assessment is conducted over the phone by your local Area Agency on Aging
Priority is given to those in greatest social and economic need, but no specific income limits apply
If your loved one receives Community Care Services or SOURCE program benefits, they may already be connected to meal delivery
Meals can extend to spouse or household members if it supports maintaining the eligible senior in their home

faqs.question: Can my loved one get meals on weekends too?
faqs.answer: Most providers deliver Monday through Friday only. Some may offer frozen or shelf-stable meals for weekends, but this varies by your local Area Agency on Aging and provider. Ask specifically about weekend coverage when you apply.

faqs.question: What happens after my loved one receives 20 meals?
faqs.answer: After 20 consecutive meals, your loved one will be reassessed for continued need. They may be placed on a waiting list to make room for others in crisis, or referred to other nutrition programs like congregate meals or food assistance.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one if they have dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of aging loved ones who cannot manage the process themselves. The Area Agency on Aging will work with you to complete the functional assessment based on your observations of your loved one's abilities.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's income matter for eligibility?
faqs.answer: There are no specific income limits, but priority is given to those in greatest social and economic need. Higher-income seniors may still qualify if they're homebound and can't safely shop or prepare meals.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get meals while also receiving SNAP benefits?
faqs.answer: Yes, home-delivered meals don't affect SNAP eligibility or benefits. These are separate programs that can work together to address food security for seniors.

faqs.question: What if there's no meal delivery provider in my loved one's rural area?
faqs.answer: Coverage varies by county and provider capacity. Contact your Area Agency on Aging to ask about alternative options like frozen meal delivery, transportation to congregate meal sites, or connections to local food pantries.

faqs.question: How much should my loved one contribute for the meals?
faqs.answer: Meals are provided at no charge, but voluntary contributions are welcomed and help sustain the program. There's no required amount, contribute what you can afford or nothing at all.

faqs.question: Can meals be customized for diabetes or other medical conditions?
faqs.answer: Providers follow USDA dietary guidelines and may offer some accommodations for common conditions like diabetes. Discuss specific dietary needs during your assessment, some providers are better equipped for medical diets than others.

programs.name: Caregiver Programs (Out-of-Home Respite Care)
programs.tagline: Temporary licensed facility care for your loved one so you can rest and recharge

localEntities.name: Georgia Center for Resources and Support

programs.intro:
If you're caring for an aging loved one with disabilities or chronic illness, Georgia's out-of-home respite programs can provide temporary care in licensed facilities while you take a break. Your loved one receives professional supervision, personal care assistance, and help with daily activities like bathing and dressing, giving you hours or overnight relief from caregiving responsibilities.

Most out-of-home respite comes through Georgia's Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) waivers, including the Elderly and Disabled Waiver Program (EDWP) for aging adults. Services range from 4-hour blocks to up to 18 hours per week in special cases, delivered in licensed facilities or approved provider homes. Because this isn't a single program but rather a service delivered through multiple pathways, eligibility and benefits vary significantly by which program your loved one qualifies for.

summary: Enrolled in Georgia Medicaid HCBS waiver
Parent has disability, chronic illness, or age-related condition
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Parent must qualify for Medicaid HCBS waiver services, which requires meeting Nursing Facility Level of Care (a clinical assessment that your loved one needs daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Parent must be enrolled in Georgia Medicaid HCBS waivers (Comprehensive Supports, New Options, or Elderly and Disabled Waiver Program)
Parent has disabilities, chronic illnesses, or age-related conditions requiring care
You serve as the primary caregiver and need temporary relief
Some programs assess caregiver stress levels for priority placement

applicationGuide.summary: Apply through your loved one's Medicaid waiver provider or contact specialized programs directly, processing varies by program type.

steps.title: Determine your pathway
steps.description: If your loved one has Medicaid HCBS waiver services, contact their current waiver provider first. If they don't have waiver services, they'll need to apply for Medicaid and waiver enrollment first.

steps.title: Contact the appropriate program
steps.description: For adoptive medically fragile children: Call Georgia Center for Resources and Support at 1-866-272-7368 or visit www.gacrs.org. For Southern Georgia: Contact Easterseals at 229-439-7061 or info@essga.org.

steps.title: Complete assessment
steps.description: Programs will assess your loved one's care needs and your caregiver stress level. Have medical documentation and income information ready.

steps.title: Submit required documentation
steps.description: Provide proof of Medicaid waiver enrollment, medical provider statements, and income/family size details as requested by the specific program.

applicationGuide.processingTime: DFCS adoptive program: up to 1 year approval timeframe; other programs vary by provider
applicationGuide.waitlist: Priority based on caregiver stress assessment; wait times vary by local provider capacity
applicationGuide.tip: Contact multiple providers in your area as availability and wait times can vary significantly between organizations.

urls.label: Georgia Center for Resources and Support

documentsNeeded: Proof of parent's Medicaid HCBS waiver enrollment
Medical provider statement documenting parent's medical condition or fragility
Recent income statements for all household members
Documentation of family size and household composition
Caregiver stress or burden assessment (may be completed during application)
For DFCS program: Adoption assistance documentation
For DFCS program: Proof that child was placed through DFCS custody
Parent's Social Security card and Medicare information
Current medication list and medical history
Emergency contact information for family members
Insurance information for parent
Legal guardianship or power of attorney documents if applicable

contacts.label: Georgia Center for Resources and Support
contacts.description: DFCS respite program for adoptive medically fragile children

contacts.label: Easterseals Southern Georgia
contacts.description: Respite services in Southern Georgia region

contacts.label: Georgia Medicaid Customer Service
contacts.description: Questions about HCBS waiver enrollment and eligibility

applicationNotes: Out-of-home respite specifically requires licensed facilities, not all respite programs offer this option, so confirm when calling
DFCS program is limited to adoptive children who are medically fragile and receive adoption assistance, very specific criteria
If your loved one isn't already on a Medicaid waiver, that process must be completed first, which can add months to the timeline
Priority placement often depends on caregiver stress levels and crisis situations, be honest about your caregiving burden during assessments

faqs.question: Can my loved one get out-of-home respite if they're not on a Medicaid waiver yet?
faqs.answer: Generally no, most out-of-home respite requires enrollment in Georgia's Medicaid HCBS waivers first. Your loved one would need to apply for Medicaid, meet Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements, and get approved for waiver services. This process can take several months.

faqs.question: What's the difference between out-of-home and in-home respite?
faqs.answer: Out-of-home respite takes your loved one to a licensed facility or approved provider's home for care, while in-home brings a caregiver to your loved one's house. Out-of-home gives you complete time away from caregiving, but your loved one must be able to adapt to a new environment temporarily.

faqs.question: How many hours of out-of-home respite can my loved one get per month?
faqs.answer: It varies significantly by program. The DFCS program offers up to 5 hours monthly at $6/hour for the first child. Other programs typically offer 4-hour blocks, with up to 18 hours weekly in special cases like ALS. Your waiver provider will determine specific allocations.

faqs.question: Can I choose which licensed facility my loved one goes to for respite?
faqs.answer: Your choices depend on which facilities are approved by your loved one's waiver provider and have availability. Ask your waiver provider for a list of approved out-of-home respite locations in your area and visit them beforehand if possible.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one has a medical emergency during out-of-home respite?
faqs.answer: Licensed respite facilities are required to have emergency protocols and staff trained in basic medical response. They'll contact emergency services if needed and notify you immediately. Make sure the facility has current emergency contacts and medical information.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist for out-of-home respite services?
faqs.answer: Wait times aren't specified in official sources and vary by local provider capacity and your priority level based on caregiver stress assessment. Contact multiple providers in your area as availability differs significantly between organizations.

faqs.question: Can I apply for both in-home and out-of-home respite at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, many waiver programs offer both options, though your total allocated respite hours may be shared between the two. Discuss with your waiver provider about combining services to maximize your relief time.

faqs.question: What if my loved one refuses to go to out-of-home respite?
faqs.answer: This is common, especially for parents with dementia or those resistant to change. Start with short visits, involve your loved one in choosing the facility if possible, and consider whether in-home respite might be a better fit initially. The facility may have strategies to help with transitions.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.tagline: Part-time paid work experience at nonprofits and public agencies to help your loved one age 55+ transition back into employment.

localEntities.name: AARP Foundation: 1718 Peachtree Street NW, Suite 991, Atlanta, GA 30309 (Cobb, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett)
WorkSource Georgia Career Centers for referrals

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and struggling to find work, SCSEP provides part-time paid employment (average 20 hours per week) at nonprofits, schools, hospitals, and senior centers while they develop job skills. They earn at least minimum wage ($7.25/hour in most Georgia areas) for up to 1,300 hours per year while receiving job training, resume help, and placement assistance to transition to unsubsidized employment.

To qualify, your loved one's total household income must be at or below 125% of federal poverty level, $19,563/year for a single person or $26,519 for a couple in 2025. Because spots are limited (only 236 participants statewide in 2020), priority goes to veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, and rural residents. Not available in all counties, coverage depends on which provider serves your area.

otherRequirements: Currently unemployed and seeking to re-enter the workforce
Poor employment prospects based on assessment
Reside in a county served by a Georgia SCSEP provider

applicationGuide.summary: Call your regional provider directly, processing varies by area and current openings.

steps.title: Find your provider
steps.description: Metro Atlanta (Cobb, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett): Call AARP Foundation at 1-855-850-2525 Monday-Friday 9am-6pm ET. Northwest Georgia: Call ACC Aging at (706) 549-4850. Other areas: Use the AARP locator at my.aarpfoundation.org/locator/scsep/ or contact WorkSource Georgia.

steps.title: Complete application and income verification
steps.description: Submit SCSEP application with proof of age, income for all household members, residency, and veteran/disability status if applicable. Provider will assess employment prospects and determine priority level.

steps.title: Assessment and placement
steps.description: If accepted, provider will match your loved one with a community service host site and develop an Individual Employment Plan with training goals and job search assistance.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible due to limited spots, varies by region and provider
applicationGuide.tip: Priority goes to veterans, those over 65, people with disabilities, and rural residents, mention these if they apply to your loved one.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID or driver's license
Proof of age (birth certificate or passport)
Proof of residence (utility bill or lease agreement)
Income documentation for all household members (Social Security award letters, pay stubs, tax returns)
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Veteran status documentation (DD-214 discharge papers if applicable)
Disability documentation if claiming priority status

contacts.label: AARP Foundation SCSEP
contacts.description: Metro Atlanta counties (Cobb, DeKalb, Douglas, Fulton, Gwinnett)

contacts.label: ACC Aging SCSEP
contacts.description: Northwest Georgia counties

contacts.label: WorkSource Georgia
contacts.description: General employment services and SCSEP provider referrals

applicationNotes: Georgia stopped administering SCSEP directly as of July 1, 2025, you must contact regional providers like AARP or ACC Aging, not state agencies
Each provider serves different counties, use the online locator to confirm your area is covered before applying
Limited enrollment means waitlists are common, ask about current availability and expected wait times when you call

faqs.question: How long can my loved one stay in the SCSEP program?
faqs.answer: Up to 1,300 hours per year maximum, which equals about 25 hours per week for 52 weeks or 65 weeks at 20 hours. Because this is a training program designed to transition to unsubsidized employment, participants are expected to actively job search and move to permanent employment when ready.

faqs.question: Can my loved one collect Social Security while working in SCSEP?
faqs.answer: Yes, but they need to report SCSEP wages to Social Security Administration. If your loved one is under full retirement age, earnings may reduce Social Security benefits if they exceed annual limits ($23,400 in 2025). Contact SSA at (800) 772-1213 to understand how SCSEP wages will affect their specific benefits.

faqs.question: What if my loved one lives in a county that doesn't have SCSEP coverage?
faqs.answer: Use the AARP locator at my.aarpfoundation.org/locator/scsep/ to confirm availability in your county. If no provider serves your area, contact WorkSource Georgia at 2-1-1 for alternative employment and training programs that may be available.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's spouse's income count toward the income limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, SCSEP counts total household income including spouse's Social Security, pensions, and any other income sources. For 2025, a married couple cannot exceed $26,519 combined annual income to qualify for the program.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply to multiple SCSEP providers to increase their chances?
faqs.answer: No, you should only apply to the provider that serves your county. Each provider has exclusive service areas, for example, AARP Foundation only serves metro Atlanta counties while ACC Aging covers northwest Georgia counties.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one gets a job offer while waiting for SCSEP?
faqs.answer: Take the job, that's the ultimate goal. SCSEP is specifically for unemployed individuals seeking to re-enter the workforce. If the job doesn't work out later and they meet income requirements again, they can reapply to SCSEP.

programs.name: Elderly Legal Assistance Program (ELAP)
programs.tagline: Free legal help for Georgians 60+ facing civil issues like benefit denials, Medicare appeals, elder abuse, or housing problems.

localEntities.name: River Valley (16 counties): Columbus office of Georgia Legal Services[3]

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and facing a civil legal issue that threatens their well-being, like a SNAP denial, Medicare appeal, housing problem, or elder abuse, Georgia's Elderly Legal Assistance Program provides free legal representation through contracted law firms statewide. Because federal law prohibits income and asset testing for this program, any Georgia resident 60+ qualifies regardless of their financial situation.

The program prioritizes cases where circumstances put your loved one's life or well-being at risk, particularly targeting vulnerable populations including low-income seniors, rural residents, and those with limited English proficiency. Services include legal advice, community education, brief consultation, and full case representation in priority areas like public benefits, health care access, Social Security appeals, consumer fraud, advance directives, and guardianship defense.

otherRequirements: Must be Georgia resident
Civil legal issue in priority areas (public benefits, health care, housing, consumer issues, elder abuse, Social Security, Medicare appeals)
Circumstances that put life or well-being at risk
No criminal cases or fee-generating matters

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your county's ELAP law firm directly by phone, services are often provided the same day for urgent matters.

steps.title: Find your county's ELAP provider
steps.description: Go to aging.georgia.gov/tools-resources/elderly-legal-assistance-program to find the law firm serving your county, or call the Aging and Disability Resource Connection at (866) 552-4464.

steps.title: Call your local provider
steps.description: Contact your county's law firm directly. Examples: Atlanta area (404) 657-5258, Coastal region 1-888-220-8399, River Valley region 1-800-615-4379. Have details about the legal issue ready.

steps.title: Describe the legal problem
steps.description: Explain how the civil legal issue affects your loved one's well-being. Priority goes to cases involving benefit denials, housing threats, elder abuse, or health care access problems.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Services often provided same day by phone; Georgia Senior Legal Hotline callbacks within 2 business days
applicationGuide.tip: For urgent situations involving active benefit terminations or housing threats, emphasize the emergency nature when you call.

contacts.label: Aging and Disability Resource Connection
contacts.description: Statewide helpline to find your county's ELAP provider

contacts.label: Georgia Senior Legal Hotline
contacts.description: Brief legal advice and referrals (separate from full ELAP representation)

contacts.label: Atlanta ELAP Office
contacts.description: ELAP services for Atlanta metro area

contacts.label: Coastal Georgia Legal Services
contacts.description: ELAP services for coastal region

faqs.question: Can my loved one get help with both a SNAP denial and a Medicare appeal at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, because ELAP covers multiple priority areas including public benefits and Medicare appeals. The same law firm can often handle related cases for your loved one. Call your county's ELAP provider to discuss all pending legal issues at once.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's legal issue doesn't seem life-threatening but is causing stress?
faqs.answer: ELAP prioritizes cases where circumstances put well-being at risk, which includes financial security and housing stability. Consumer fraud, debt collection, or benefit denials often qualify even if not immediately life-threatening. Call your county provider to discuss, they can determine if your loved one's situation meets priority criteria.

faqs.question: Is there a difference between ELAP and the Georgia Senior Legal Hotline?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Georgia Senior Legal Hotline at (404) 389-9992 provides brief advice and referrals only. ELAP provides full legal representation and advocacy through local law firms. For complex cases needing ongoing representation, you want ELAP through your county provider.

stateOverview.intro: Georgia offers 10 major programs to help your aging loved one, 7 financial benefits that require income qualification, 2 free resources available to everyone, and 1 employment program. These range from covering home care costs (Community Care Services Program can pay for personal care to avoid nursing home placement) to reducing monthly expenses (Medicare Savings Programs can cut Medicare premiums by $175+ per month). Most benefits require income below $2,000-3,000 per month for a single person, but qualification rules vary significantly by program. Start by checking the highest-impact benefits first, home care assistance and Medicare cost reduction, since these typically provide the largest monthly savings for Georgia families.

startHere.name: Community Care Services Program (CCSP)
startHere.why: Can cover thousands in monthly home care costs to keep your loved one out of nursing homes while on Medicaid.

startHere.name: Georgia Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
startHere.why: Reduces Medicare premiums and costs for anyone with income below $1,660/month.

startHere.name: Georgia State Health Insurance Assistance Program (Georgia SHIP)
startHere.why: Free Medicare counseling available to everyone, start here to avoid costly plan mistakes.

byNeed.description: Both programs help your loved one stay home instead of entering a nursing home by covering personal care and homemaker services.

programs: Georgia Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
byNeed.description: Help paying Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments for qualifying low-income seniors.

programs: Senior Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (Senior SNAP)
byNeed.description: Monthly grocery assistance or delivered meals for seniors who can't shop or cook safely.
Temporary licensed facility care so you can take breaks from caregiving.

programs: Georgia State Health Insurance Assistance Program (Georgia SHIP)
byNeed.description: Free Medicare counseling and legal help for benefit denials, elder abuse, or housing problems.
Free energy improvements to reduce your loved one's monthly utility bills.

quickFacts: Most Georgia benefit programs require income below $2,000-3,000/month for a single person, but limits vary by program
Georgia SHIP and legal assistance are free resources, no income limits or applications required
Many programs have waiting lists, especially home care services, so apply early even if your loved one doesn't need help immediately
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Georgia offers two types of help: qualification-based benefits that require income limits (like CCSP home care or SNAP grocery assistance) and free resources available to everyone regardless of income. Georgia SHIP provides free Medicare counseling to any senior, while the Elderly Legal Assistance Program offers free legal help to anyone 60+ facing benefit denials or elder abuse, no financial qualification needed.
